title: Modi Raises Death of Three Indian Seafarers at G7, Asserts Maritime Safety as Sovereign Interest
summary: At the G7 summit in Évian, PM Modi called for the protection of maritime trade routes after three Indian seafarers were killed aboard the tanker Settebello, struck by US forces during the Hormuz blockade in February. India supplies the world's largest maritime labour force — over 2.5 lakh sailors crewing roughly 12% of global shipping — and Modi raised the deaths directly with world leaders, including in a bilateral meeting with Trump, while both sides emphasised defence and trade cooperation.

coverages.headline: Modi raises maritime safety, seafarer deaths at G7 Évian summit
coverages.excerpt: Modi raised the deaths of three Indian seafarers killed when the tanker Settebello was struck during the Hormuz blockade directly with world leaders at the G7, while also holding a bilateral meeting with Trump emphasising defence and trade cooperation.
coverages.bias_notes: This story sits at the intersection of three narratives that are usually covered separately: the Hormuz blockade's human cost, India's economic stake in global shipping, and Modi's diplomatic positioning at a summit where India is a guest rather than a member. The 12% global shipping crew figure is a genuinely underappreciated fact about India's economic leverage — it means India has a direct human and economic stake in maritime security disputes that doesn't require any territorial claim or treaty alliance to justify. What deserves more scrutiny: whether raising this at the G7 (a forum India doesn't have voting power in) achieves anything beyond rhetorical positioning, and what concrete commitment, if any, the US or other G7 members made in response to the deaths of nationals killed by US forces during the blockade — a detail with real accountability implications that easily gets lost inside diplomatic framing of 'protecting maritime routes.'
